mnemonics.title: Hessian Classification
mnemonics.content: MIN-MAX-SADDLE: If Eigenvalues are All Positive → MIN, All Negative → MAX, Mixed signs → SADDLE (MMSA)

mnemonics.title: Principal Minor Test
mnemonics.content: For Positive Definite: Determinants must DELIGHT (D₁ > 0, D₂ > 0, ..., Dₙ > 0)

mnemonics.title: Hessian Components
mnemonics.content: Hessian has HESS: Second Derivatives, Eigenvalues tell nature, Symmetric when continuous, Summation of curvature

acronyms.usage: Used to remember Hessian characterizes stability of critical points in optimization

acronyms.expansion: Minimum: Hessian Positive Definite; Maximum: Hessian Negative Definite
Signs Alternating Definitively D/L (Definite and Indefinite = SADDLE)
acronyms.usage: Remember saddle points arise when Hessian has mixed eigenvalue signs

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a bowl-shaped surface (paraboloid opening upward) - any direction from minimum goes up. Eigenvalues all point upward.
Imagine an inverted bowl (paraboloid opening downward) - any direction from maximum goes down. Eigenvalues all point downward.
Imagine a Pringles chip surface - goes up in one direction, down in another. Curvature differs based on direction.
Each eigenvalue of Hessian represents curvature in the direction of corresponding eigenvector - like second derivative in each principal direction.

analogies.analogy: Like a 3D terrain map - the Hessian tells you the slope (gradient) and the steepness/curvature (second derivatives) in every direction at every point.
Like standing at a mountain summit, valley floor, or mountain pass - summit (max), valley (min), pass (saddle) determined by terrain shape.
Like a bowl - no matter which way you roll the ball, it rolls to the bottom (minimum). All curvature points upward.
Like measuring curvature in different directions on a curved surface - some directions steeper, some flatter, some curving downward.
